Sample Digest · Monday 27 April 2026

A recent Pathology digest,
as sent to subscribers

This is the real Monday digest from Monday 27 April 2026. Showing the first 8 of 23 articles from that week — every new paper from the top journals in Pathology.

23articles that week
5journals covered
£0for your first 4 weeks
Note: Summaries are AI-generated from publicly available PubMed abstracts. Always verify against the source article before clinical use.
Histopathology

Glomerular plasmalemma vesicle-associated protein-1 as an endothelial remodelling marker complementing C4d in chronic active antibody-mediated rejection

Igarashi Y, Shimokawa M, Kawanishi K et al. · 2026 Apr 21
Study Type: Retrospective cohort study
Key Question: Does glomerular plasmalemma vesicle-associated protein-1 (PV-1) complement C4d staining in identifying active endothelial injury in chronic active antibody-mediated rejection?
Key Findings:
  • Analysis of 429 caABMR biopsies showed PV-1 specifically identifies endothelial remodelling (particularly in double-contour lesions) while C4d reflects cumulative complement activation
  • PV-1 demonstrated dynamic reduction following B-cell-directed therapy, whereas C4d showed persistent staining in ABO-incompatible grafts
  • In multivariable Cox models, changes in PV-1 (ΔPV-1) remained independently associated with death-censored graft survival, while changes in C4d (ΔC4d) did not
Clinical Relevance: Combined PV-1 and C4d assessment could refine histopathological evaluation of renal transplant biopsies by capturing distinct aspects of microvascular pathology and potentially improving monitoring of treatment response in antibody-mediated rejection.
Limitations: Retrospective design limits ability to establish causality and standardise treatment protocols across the study period.
PubMed →DOI →
Histopathology

Insights into Langerhans cell sarcoma: Focus on cutaneous Langerhans cell sarcoma

Xiong Y, Liu W, Zhang W et al. · 2026 Apr 21
Study Type: Retrospective case series with literature review
Key Question: What are the clinicopathological features that distinguish cutaneous Langerhans cell sarcoma from extracutaneous disease?
Key Findings:
  • Cutaneous LCS frequently arises from malignant transformation of pre-existing lesions (74.4% progressed vs 13.9% de novo cases), compared to only 19.6% transformation in extracutaneous disease (P<0.001)
  • Progressed cutaneous LCS shows higher lymph node metastasis rates (56.3% vs 0%, P=0.021) and Ki-67 proliferation index compared to de novo cases
  • Langerin immunostaining can be focal (<5% of cells in one case), requiring ultrastructural confirmation of Birbeck granules for diagnosis
Clinical Relevance: UK pathologists should recognise that cutaneous LCS often represents transformation from pre-existing Langerhans cell lesions and may require electron microscopy when immunohistochemistry is equivocal.
Limitations: Very small primary cohort (n=5) from a single institution limits generalisability of findings.
PubMed →DOI →
Histopathology

World Health Organization classification of tumours of the breast 6th edition 2026

Quinn C, Tan PH, Allison KH et al. · 2026 Apr 21
Study Type: Classification system update/guidelines
Key Question: What are the key changes in the 6th edition WHO Classification of Breast Tumours that will affect diagnostic practice?
Key Findings:
  • HER2 reporting categories updated following DESTINY-Breast trials, with refined predictive testing approaches
  • New diagnostic entity: invasive lobular carcinoma with extracellular mucin, carrying prognostic significance
  • Revised criteria for malignant phyllodes tumours (now requires only 4/5 adverse histological features) and mucinous carcinoma (restricted to grade 1-2 morphology with favourable biomarkers)
Clinical Relevance: UK breast pathologists must implement these classification changes to ensure accurate diagnosis, appropriate biomarker reporting, and optimal treatment selection aligned with current therapeutic trials and NHS treatment pathways.
Limitations: As a classification update rather than research study, implementation challenges and validation of new criteria in routine practice remain to be established.
PubMed →DOI →
Histopathology

PLAG1-rearranged uterine mesenchymal tumours: A study of 11 cases showing distinct histological features beyond myxoid morphology

Cai M, Zuo K, Yu L et al. · 2026 Apr 22
Study Type: Retrospective case series
Key Question: What are the clinicopathological features and molecular characteristics of PLAG1-rearranged uterine mesenchymal tumours beyond the historically recognised myxoid morphology?
Key Findings:
  • Morphological heterogeneity with myxoid features in 54.5% (6/11), epithelioid in 27.3% (3/11), and spindle cell in 18.2% (2/11) of cases
  • Multiple fusion partners identified, most commonly TRPS1::PLAG1 (27.3%) and CSDE1::PLAG1 (18.2%)
  • Clinical significance demonstrated by 27.3% recurrence/metastasis rate during follow-up
Clinical Relevance: UK pathologists should recognise that PLAG1-rearranged uterine tumours show diverse morphologies beyond myxoid patterns, requiring molecular testing for accurate diagnosis and appropriate patient counselling regarding recurrence risk.
Limitations: Small case series (n=11) limits generalisability of findings and prognostic conclusions.
PubMed →DOI →
Histopathology

Malignant epithelioid mesenchymal neoplasms with EWSR1/FUS::CREM fusions: clinicopathological and molecular genetic analysis of seven cases highlighting immunophenotypic heterogeneity, frequent aggressive behaviour, and diagnostic pitfalls

Zhao M, Xu J, Zheng S et al. · 2026 Apr 23
Study Type: Case series
Key Question: What are the clinicopathological features and diagnostic challenges of epithelioid mesenchymal neoplasms with EWSR1/FUS::CREM fusions?
Key Findings:
  • Seven cases (age 5-55 years) showed epithelioid morphology with heterogeneous immunoprofiles that led to frequent misdiagnosis as mesothelioma, Ewing sarcoma, sex cord-stromal tumours, or metastatic carcinoma
  • Molecular analysis identified EWSR1::CREM fusions in 5 cases and FUS::CREM fusions in 2 cases, confirmed by FISH
  • All 3 patients with follow-up had aggressive clinical courses with recurrence or metastasis; 2 presented with disseminated disease
Clinical Relevance: UK pathologists should recognise this emerging sarcoma entity that mimics multiple other tumour types immunohistochemically, requiring molecular testing to avoid misdiagnosis and ensure appropriate oncological management.
Limitations: Small case series with limited follow-up data on clinical outcomes.
PubMed →DOI →
Histopathology

Aberrant p53 immunohistochemical staining is uncommon in serrated epithelial change, regardless of association with dysplasia

Bahceci D, Lauwers GY, Choi WT · 2026 Apr 23
Study Type: Retrospective cohort study
Key Question: Does p53 immunohistochemical staining differ in serrated epithelial change (SEC) associated with dysplasia versus SEC without dysplasia in IBD patients?
Key Findings:
  • Strong p53 staining was uncommon in SEC regardless of dysplasia association (13% vs 8% in controls, P=0.315)
  • SEC patients with synchronous/metachronous dysplasia were older (mean 57 vs 47 years, P=0.006), had longer disease duration (26 vs 13 years, P<0.001), and more frequently had Crohn's disease (21% vs 0%, P=0.023)
  • SEC showed more moderate p53 staining compared to normal IBD mucosa (71% vs 13%, P<0.001) but similar strong staining patterns
Clinical Relevance: This suggests p53 immunohistochemistry has limited value for risk stratifying SEC in IBD surveillance programmes, but careful colonoscopic follow-up may be warranted for high-risk SEC patients.
Limitations: The study examined p53 staining only and did not assess other molecular markers that might better characterise SEC's neoplastic potential.
PubMed →DOI →
Histopathology

Glandular lesions of the vagina: a review of a group of rare lesions

Wong RW, Talia KL, McCluggage WG · 2026 Apr 23
Study Type: Narrative review
Key Question: How should pathologists approach the diagnosis and classification of rare glandular lesions of the vagina, particularly distinguishing primary adenocarcinomas from metastases?
Key Findings:
  • Primary vaginal carcinomas represent <1% of female cancers, with adenocarcinomas being extremely rare compared to squamous cell carcinomas
  • Primary vaginal adenocarcinomas present a diagnostic challenge since normal vaginal tissue lacks glands, necessitating careful exclusion of metastatic disease
  • Multiple morphological subtypes exist, many resembling cervical adenocarcinomas, requiring systematic approach to typing and reporting
Clinical Relevance: This review provides essential guidance for UK pathologists encountering these diagnostically challenging lesions, offering practical approaches to distinguish primary from metastatic disease in routine histopathology practice.
Limitations: As a narrative review, this does not provide systematic evidence synthesis or quantitative data on diagnostic accuracy of proposed approaches.
PubMed →DOI →
Modern pathology : an official journal of the United States and Canadian Academy of Pathology, Inc

Copy Number-Low/TP53 Mutated Endometrial Cancer With Wild Type p53 Immunoexpression: Implications for Risk Stratification and Management When Using Next Generation Sequencing for Molecular Classification

Rabban JT, Wolsky RJ, Kayraklioglu N et al. · 2026 Apr 21
Study Type: Retrospective cohort study
Key Question: What are the clinical implications of a rare subgroup of endometrial cancers with TP53 mutations but low copy number alterations and wild-type p53 immunohistochemistry?
Key Findings:
  • 18/755 endometrial cancers (2.4%) showed TP53 mutation with low copy number burden, wild-type p53 IHC, and low variant allele frequency (median 13%)
  • These cases had low-grade endometrioid histology, minimal lymphovascular invasion, and favourable prognosis (6.25% recurrence rate, no deaths)
  • Standard ProMisE immunohistochemistry-based classification fails to identify this subgroup due to wild-type p53 staining pattern
Clinical Relevance: UK pathologists using NGS-based molecular classification should consider copy number analysis when TP53 mutations have <50% variant allele frequency, as standard immunohistochemistry may misclassify these cases with potentially different treatment implications.
Limitations: Small case series with limited follow-up data insufficient to establish definitive prognostic significance.
PubMed →DOI →
